Framework · free to use

An AI usage policy your team will actually read.

Most AI policies are 14 pages of risk-team prose nobody reads. This is a one-page policy framework built for B2B companies between $5M and $50M — the kind that fits on a poster, gets adopted, and still covers the things that matter.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
Use this template

Customize and ship in one afternoon

Copy the seven sections below into your own document. Replace the bracketed examples with your actual tools, owners, and data categories. Get the document under one page.

The single best predictor that an AI policy will be followed: it fits on one page. The second best: it names a specific human to ask when in doubt. Almost nothing else matters.

Section 1

Purpose & scope

Sample text: This policy covers how [Company] employees and contractors may use AI tools (including Claude, ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, and similar) in the course of their work. It applies to anyone with a [Company] email address. Questions about anything not covered here go to [Name, Title].

Section 2

Approved tools

  • Primary: [Claude Team / ChatGPT Team / etc.]. Provisioned by IT; all employees get a seat.
  • Secondary, with approval: [tool], [tool] — request access from [Name].
  • Not approved: free public versions of any AI tool for work involving customer, employee, or financial data. Personal accounts must not be used for [Company] data.
Section 3

Data classifications

CategoryExamplesOK in approved AI tools?
PublicMarketing copy, published content, public regulatory filingsYes
InternalProcess docs, internal memos, training materialYes, with approved tools only
ConfidentialCustomer data, contracts, financial detailYes, only in enterprise-tier tools with no-training settings confirmed
RegulatedPHI, payment card data, attorney-client privileged contentOnly via tools and configurations explicitly approved for that data class
Section 4

What you must do

  • Review AI output before using it. You are responsible for what you ship, not the tool.
  • Cite or disclose AI assistance when (a) a client asks, (b) you are submitting work to a regulated authority, or (c) the work is published under your individual byline.
  • Use the approved enterprise tools when working with any data not classified Public.
  • Tell [Name] if you see a useful new pattern — we want to share it.
Section 5

What you must not do

  • Paste customer data, contracts, or financial detail into free public AI tools.
  • Use AI to produce work you cannot or do not review before delivering.
  • Use AI to generate fake quotes, fake citations, or fake evidence.
  • Make hiring, firing, performance review, or credit decisions based on AI output alone.
Section 6

When to escalate

Sample text: If you are unsure whether something fits this policy, ask [Name, Title] before doing it. There is no penalty for asking. There is a penalty for assuming.

Section 7

Review

Sample text: This policy is reviewed every 6 months by [Name] and [Name]. Material changes are communicated in the [weekly all-hands]. The current version always lives at [link].

Common additions

Optional sections by industry

  • Healthcare: add an explicit BAA-required section. List the specific platforms covered by your BAA.
  • Legal: add a privileged-information section and a courtesy-disclosure rule for AI-assisted drafts.
  • Financial services: add a section pointing to your firm's existing model risk and disclosure policies.
  • Public sector / government contracting: add a section on whether AI use must be disclosed in deliverables under contract terms.
